entities.description: A public TEI-like digital text keyed from Hitchcock's 1921 public-domain edition, including the edition title page, editor's summary, surviving Donet text, page and folio milestones, and embedded editorial notes.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00008
notes: The collection history says the 2016-2018 batch was manually keyed and proofread from public-domain editions. The 2016 review date and phase-3 location strongly associate CME00008 with that batch, but no item-level production log was located.

bibliographic.citation: Reginald Pecock, The Donet, now first edited from MS. Bodl. 916 and collated with The Poore Mennis Myrrour (British Museum, Addl. 37788), ed. Elsie Vaughan Hitchcock, Early English Text Society, Original Series 156 (London: Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press, 1921).
bibliographic.publisher: Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press, for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 156
bibliographic.title: The Donet

entities.label: Hitchcock's 1921 edition of The Donet
notes: The surviving Donet text occupies printed pp. 1-214. The frontispiece, preface, introduction, notes, Poore Mennis Myrrour appendix, biblical-reference list, glossary, and index are separate reproduction or editorial matter.
The title page says 'now first edited,' supporting 1921 as the first printed edition of the complete surviving work.

rights.basis: U-M expressly marks the CME materials derived from this edition public domain in the United States. The edition was published in 1921, and U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A explains the 95-year maximum term for works already under statutory protection before 1978; no contrary U.S. status was found.
rights.component: The 1921 transcription, introduction, editorial notes, appendices, glossary, and index
notes: This U.S. assessment does not decide every other jurisdiction or the contractual terms of a particular scan provider.

access.accessMethod: Exact shelfmark, title, author, MARCO, Medieval Manuscripts, and Digital Bodleian data-API searches
notes: MARCO explicitly says 'Not available online.' Digital Bodleian returned no author or title match for this item. This date-bounded result does not prove that no internal preservation photography or unindexed surrogate exists.
The selected fol. 3v plate in the 1921 edition is recorded separately and must not be mistaken for a complete facsimile.

access.accessMethod: Register for Bodleian Archives & Manuscripts Requests, request MS. Bodl. 916 from its catalog record, and consult it in the Weston Library Level 1 reading room subject to preservation and access review
Email the completed mediated-copying form to mediated.copying@bodleian.ox.ac.uk and request the complete physical codex, not only the written Donet folios
access.format: Reference-quality PDF scans supplied by file transfer, if the entire object is suitable for copying

localCopies.label: Bodleian Libraries mediated copying order form

notes: Specify front and back covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all preliminary and final flyleaves, every recto and verso including fols. 1r-106v, all blanks and shortened leaves, and visible missing-leaf stubs or inserts. Ask for the authoritative image sequence and foliation.
Map The Donet separately to fols. 1r-106v; do not treat the catalog-inferred 1r-109v range as a verified text boundary.
The current form lists GBP 1 per scan and a GBP 10 minimum for non-Oxford users, but the item is subject to conservation, legal, and capacity review and needs an item-specific quote.
Reference-copy supply does not authorize Git redistribution or commercial print-on-demand use.

rights.basis: The current mediated-copying form says scans of out-of-copyright material are copyright Bodleian Libraries and supplied under CC BY-NC 4.0; the service and terms preserve item-level and use-specific restrictions.
rights.component: Any new reference scans supplied through Bodleian mediated copying
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
notes: A repository supporting for-profit or otherwise commercial print-on-demand activity cannot assume that CC BY-NC authorizes repository redistribution or downstream commercial use. Obtain written item-specific permission first.
The medieval text's age does not erase terms attached to newly supplied image files.

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: second half of the fifteenth century

entities.description: The sole known surviving manuscript of The Donet, a parchment codex copied fair in the second half of the fifteenth century and corrected by another hand. The Donet text fills the 106 written folios; blank and shortened leaves follow it.

holding.notes: Thomas Allen gave the manuscript to the Bodleian collection in 1601; the old Arch. B. I. designation remains on fol. 1r.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 916
notes: No public complete or partial Digital Bodleian object for this shelfmark was located as of 2026-07-11; MARCO explicitly labels it not available online.
Hitchcock's 1921 volume reproduces only fol. 3v as a monochrome frontispiece. A complete reproduction request must not treat that plate as manuscript coverage.

physicalDescription.extent: Current catalog: iii + 109 leaves; Hitchcock: 106 written folios followed by blank and shortened leaves
notes: The exact written work runs fols. 1r-106v; the current msItem locus 1r-109v is marked inferred and extends through blank matter.
Two leaves are missing after fol. 76 and two after fol. 82. Hitchcock also identifies a smaller discontinuity between fols. 56v and 57r.
Spaces for illuminated initials remain unfilled, with guide letters visible. Red writing marks selected structural sentences.
The manuscript was written fair by one apparently consistent scribe and corrected by another hand; later marginal notes and ownership additions also survive.

rights.basis: The work and witness are fifteenth-century, and U-M expressly marks its modern public transcription No Copyright-United States.
rights.component: The fifteenth-century Donet text embodied in the manuscript
notes: This concerns the historical text, not ownership of the physical object, modern photography, catalog text, or image-service terms.

entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century miscellany whose first textual component, The Poore Mennis Myrrour on fols. 3r-63v, is Pecock's separate abridgement of Part I of the Donet. Hitchcock used its parallel wording for collation but did not treat it as a copy of the complete Donet.

historical_designations: Amherst manuscript sold as lot 676 in 1909
holding.notes: Acquired by the British Museum in 1909; the current British Library component record is authoritative for the shelfmark and access route.

entities.label: British Library Add MS 37788
notes: The relevant association to CME00008 is the related abridgement on fols. 3r-63v, not a claim that The Donet itself occupies those leaves.

physicalDescription.extent: iv + 80 leaves in Hitchcock's cited British Museum catalog description
notes: The Poore Mennis Myrrour occupies fols. 3r-63v; fol. 15r is blank and partially cut, but belongs within its sequence.
The remaining leaves contain other English and Latin theological, moral, poetic, and practical material, so a work-only excerpt would not be a complete codex facsimile.

relations.subjectLabel: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00008

scope.notes: The text is interrupted by lost leaves after fols. 76 and 82 and a smaller discontinuity between 56v and 57r. Hitchcock expands abbreviations, adds punctuation, supplies and emends readings in brackets, and documents manuscript forms in notes.

object.description: The 106 written folios carrying The Donet

scope.notes: The relation records Hitchcock's editorial comparison. It does not make Add MS 37788 a witness of the complete Donet or a source for Part II.

subject.description: Part I parallel passages printed with Poore Mennis Myrrour variants and Appendix I

scope.notes: The plate does not reproduce the rest of the codex, its binding, blanks, missing-leaf stubs, rubrication in color, or other surfaces.

relations.subjectLabel: Hitchcock frontispiece of MS. Bodl. 916, fol. 3v
Bodleian TEI catalog description of MS. Bodl. 916

reviewNotes: The Donet, The Poore Mennis Myrrour, both physical codices, the 1921 edition, the U-M encoding, and the repository artifact are distinct layers.
The exact Donet text occupies MS. Bodl. 916 fols. 1r-106v. The current catalog's fols. 1r-109v locus is explicitly inferred for the sole msItem and includes blank matter after the written text.
The Poore Mennis Myrrour is a separate authorial abridgement of Part I, not a second witness of the complete Donet; Add MS 37788 is therefore a collation source rather than a Donet base witness.
Public access, local caching, copyright, image-service terms, and permission to redistribute or use images commercially are recorded separately.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00008 XML is a local repository representation of University of Michigan CME item CME00008, manually keyed and proofread from Elsie Vaughan Hitchcock's 1921 EETS edition. Hitchcock transcribed the sole surviving Donet witness, Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 916, and collated parallel Part I passages against Pecock's separate abridgement The Poore Mennis Myrrour in British Library Add MS 37788. A complete scan of the 1921 edition and its scan metadata are cached. No public complete facsimile of either manuscript was found: Bodleian's current record explicitly says MS. Bodl. 916 is not available online, while the edition exposes only one monochrome manuscript plate, fol. 3v. Reproduction routes therefore request each whole physical codex and state the relevant textual portion separately.

catalogSummary.author: Reginald Pecock
catalogSummary.date: c. 1445; composed within the broader range 1443-1449
catalogSummary.region: Mainly East Midland language; exact place of composition unresolved
catalogSummary.title: The Donet

catalogingSubject.label: The Donet
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Reginald Pecock's two-part Middle English prose dialogue introducing and summarizing Christian doctrine, ethics, prayer, and meditation. The subject includes the work's prologue and two parts as preserved in Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 916, but excludes that physical codex, Hitchcock's 1921 edition and apparatus, the separate authorial abridgement The Poore Mennis Myrrour, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Prologue
contentParts.summary: Explains the Donet as an introduction, compendium, and complement to the Rule of Christian Religion; defines its father-and-son dialogue form and two-part plan; explains the title; defends writing in English; and asks readers to judge the author's intended meaning fairly.

contentParts.label: Part I
contentParts.summary: Eighteen chapters introduce the soul, bodily and rational faculties, natural and moral philosophy, the four tables of moral virtues, the seven matters necessary to Christian living, and related instruction on law, virtue, vice, belief, and conduct.

contentParts.label: Part II
notes: The witness lacks four leaves in its second part and probably loses at least one chapter heading. Hitchcock therefore questions the numbering of chapters 14-18; the 23 XML containers describe the surviving edited structure, not a secure count of the originally composed chapters.
contentParts.summary: Twenty-three surviving chapter containers develop further truths within the seven matters, defend the work's arrangement, and culminate in practical instruction on praise, prayer, meditation, and disciplined Christian activity.

assertion.notes: Hitchcock argues that authorized publication followed the Rule of Christian Religion after 1443 and probably preceded work on the Repressor in 1449, while acknowledging that no exact year can be assigned. The Middle English Dictionary conventionally labels the work c. 1445.

dateStatements.display: c. 1445; probably composed and authorized within 1443-1449
Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 916, second half of the fifteenth century

scope.description: The sole known surviving manuscript copy

extent.description: The surviving work is organized as one prose prologue, eighteen Part I chapter containers, and twenty-three Part II chapter containers. The treatise division of CME00008.xml contains 642 P, 214 PB, 2,299 NOTE, and 14,114 HI elements and no L or LG elements. These are encoding metrics. Four leaves are missing from Part II, probably including a chapter heading, so 41 surviving chapter containers must not be treated as the certain original chapter count.

formStatements.description: A sustained didactic prose dialogue between a son asking questions and a father answering. Brief Latin phrases and quoted formulas do not make the work mixed-form.

assertion.notes: The work teaches through a father-and-son question-and-answer frame, though its extended arguments are more elaborate than a short elementary catechism.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Brief embedded Latin does not make the work a bilingual parallel text.

scope.description: The opening invocation, technical terminology, work titles, and scriptural or learned phrases embedded in the Middle English prose

notes: The current Bodleian TEI locus 1r-109v is marked type='inferred' because the codex has one msItem. Hitchcock directly describes 106 written folios plus blank matter, and the encoded folio milestones end at 106v; the exact work-text mapping is therefore fols. 1r-106v while the broader inferred catalog locus is retained as a competing catalog assertion.
The Poore Mennis Myrrour is modeled as a related authorial abridgement and collation source, not as a witness of the complete Donet.
Rights in the medieval work, the 1921 edition, U-M encoding, Internet Archive scan, selected manuscript plate, and any future commissioned Bodleian images remain separate in the lineage manifest.

next_steps: Consult recent Pecock chronology and dialect scholarship that distinguishes draft circulation, revision, and authorized publication.
Keep the work's composition place separate from the later witness's English origin and present Oxford holding.
openQuestions.question: Can the Donet's authorized composition or publication date and original place of composition be established more narrowly than c. 1445, 1443-1449, and an East Midland linguistic association?

next_steps: Compare the Donet's internal cross-references, the Rule of Christian Religion, and The Poore Mennis Myrrour against the surviving sequence.
Do not normalize Hitchcock's questioned Part II chapter numbers into a certain original count.
openQuestions.question: Exactly how much text and how many chapter boundaries were lost with the four missing leaves in Part II?

next_steps: Request the complete physical codex from Bodleian Imaging Services or mediated copying, including covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, stubs, and every recto and verso, rather than ordering only the Donet text.
If supplied, cache the complete deliverable and map the work text to fols. 1r-106v, retaining any different authoritative image or folio sequence.
Ask for written permission before redistributing supplied images in the Git repository or using them in commercial print-on-demand products.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete current image sequence of MS. Bodl. 916 confirm every physical boundary, blank, missing-leaf stub, and the difference between the catalog-inferred fols. 1r-109v locus and Hitchcock's fols. 1r-106v written text?

assertion.notes: A dialect region does not establish an exact place of composition, and the surviving copy may mix authorial and scribal features.

notes: Do not infer composition at Oxford merely from the proposed Oxford dialect or the manuscript's present Bodleian location.

scope.description: The linguistic character of Pecock's work as represented by the sole later copy

notes: The current Bodleian catalog identifies Pecock as author. Hitchcock also supports the attribution through the work's relations to Pecock's other writings while noting that the manuscript has no direct original author heading.

scope.description: The 1921 scholarly transcription, punctuation, emendation, notes, and apparatus used by the U-M encoding
Parallel passages in Part I compared with The Poore Mennis Myrrour

summaries.value: A two-part Middle English prose dialogue in which a son questions a father about Christian religion. Pecock presents it as an introductory key, compact report, and complement to his longer Rule of Christian Religion. The prologue explains the project and title; Part I treats the soul, reason and free will, natural and moral philosophy, virtues, vices, and the seven matters of Christian living; Part II develops further doctrinal and ethical questions and closes with sustained instruction in vocal prayer and meditation. The sole surviving manuscript is imperfect, with material lost around its second part.

notes: The manuscript has no heading that directly supplies a title, but the prologue calls the dialogue a 'donet' or key, and the institutional catalog and scholarly edition use The Donet.

titles.value: Þe donet or key of goddis lawe; þe donet or key of cristen religioun

scope.description: U-M item title incorporating edition and source information

titles.value: The donet, by Reginald Pecock. Now first edited from MS. Bodl. 916 and collated with The poore mennis myrrour (British Museum, Addl. 37788) by Elsie Vaughan Hitchcock

scope.description: Opening of the English prologue after the Latin invocation

titles.value: For as moche as the book y-callid the reule of cristen religioun

work.author: Reginald Pecock
work.dateDisplay: c. 1445; composed within the broader range 1443-1449

publication.embeddedTitle: The Donet BY REGINALD PECOCK, D.D. BISHOP OF ST. ASAPH AND CHICHESTER

work.regionDisplay: Mainly East Midland language; exact place of composition unresolved
work.summary: A two-part Middle English prose dialogue in which a son questions a father about Christian religion. Pecock presents it as an introductory key, compact report, and complement to his longer Rule of Christian Religion. The prologue explains the project and title; Part I treats the soul, reason and free will, natural and moral philosophy, virtues, vices, and the seven matters of Christian living; Part II develops further doctrinal and ethical questions and closes with sustained instruction in vocal prayer and meditation. The sole surviving manuscript is imperfect, with material lost around its second part.
